claims.claim_text: Transformers replaced recurrence/convolutions with attention and were markedly more parallelizable than earlier sequence-model architectures.
claims.span_hint: lines 89-91 | section: Why Transformers Changed Language Modeling
claims.rationale: The Transformer paper abstract directly contrasts prior recurrent/convolutional models with an attention-only architecture and highlights improved parallelizability.

claims.claim_text: Relevant information buried inside long prompts can be used unreliably even by explicitly long-context models.
claims.span_hint: lines 117-119 | section: Why Transformers Changed Language Modeling
claims.rationale: Lost in the Middle directly reports degraded performance when relevant information appears in the middle of long contexts.

claims.claim_text: Instruction tuning and RLHF are the training stages that turn a base completion model into a more instruction-following chat assistant.
claims.span_hint: lines 139-142 | section: Training, Adaptation, and Knowledge Boundaries
claims.rationale: The InstructGPT paper directly describes supervised fine-tuning on demonstrations followed by reinforcement learning from human feedback to improve instruction following.

claims.claim_text: Purely parametric language models store knowledge in their weights, but provenance and timely knowledge updates remain open problems without retrieved external memory.
claims.span_hint: lines 143-153 | section: Training, Adaptation, and Knowledge Boundaries
claims.rationale: The RAG paper abstract explicitly says large language models store factual knowledge in parameters while provenance and updating world knowledge remain open problems.

claims.claim_text: The original transformer paper presented parallelization as a central advantage over older sequential architectures.
claims.span_hint: lines 354-356 | section: Did You Know?, item 1
claims.rationale: The abstract explicitly says the Transformer is more parallelizable and requires significantly less training time than prior approaches.

claims.claim_text: The module claims a broad incident pattern without an attributable source.
claims.span_hint: line 358 | section: Did You Know?, item 2
claims.anchor_text: Many production LLM incidents are context incidents rather than model incidents, because the answer can only be as grounded as the evidence and instructions supplied to the model.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Weak context assembly can cause LLM failures even when the underlying model is capable, because answers depend on the evidence and instructions supplied to the model.
claims.rationale: This sounds like an empirical claim about production incidents, but it does not name a source or dataset and should be reframed as mechanism-level guidance.

claims.claim_text: The sentence makes an unsupported comparative performance claim about small versus large models in production.
claims.span_hint: line 360 | section: Did You Know?, item 3
claims.anchor_text: A small model can outperform a larger model on a narrow production task when the prompt, schema, examples, evaluation set, and routing policy match the task well.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A smaller model may be the better production choice for a narrow task if it meets the team's evaluation threshold.
claims.rationale: The current wording asserts a broad outperform claim with specific causal conditions that are not directly backed by a matched primary source.

claims.claim_text: The sentence overstates what long-context evidence directly establishes.
claims.span_hint: line 362 | section: Did You Know?, item 4
claims.anchor_text: Long context windows change what is possible, but they do not remove the need for retrieval quality, authority labeling, source freshness, and output validation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Long context windows change what is possible, but they do not guarantee reliable use of every relevant passage in a prompt.
claims.rationale: Long-context reliability is citable, but the original sentence adds several downstream control claims that are not directly established by the long-context source alone.

further_reading.title: Attention Is All You Need
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for the transformer's attention-only architecture and the parallelization claim that anchors the module's historical framing.

further_reading.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback
further_reading.why_relevant: Best foundational reference for the module's explanation of instruction tuning, preference data, and RLHF-style chat-model adaptation.

further_reading.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
further_reading.why_relevant: Strong background for the module's retrieval-versus-parametric-memory discussion and the limits of relying only on model weights for current knowledge.

notes: Excluded the opening hypothetical incident, diagrams, code blocks, quiz items, and hands-on exercise steps. Focused on source-bound technical history/training/context claims plus the unsupported empirical-sounding Did You Know items that should be rewritten rather than cited.
